text: Feline bronchial disease (feline asthma) is a chronic inflammatory airway condition affecting approximately 1–5% of cats. It is characterized by reversible bronchoconstriction, airway hyperresponsiveness, and eosinophilic airway inflammation. Siamese and related breeds appear overrepresented. Allergen exposure, environmental irritants (cigarette smoke, dusty litter, aerosol sprays), and stress are recognized triggers. Clinical signs include episodic coughing, wheezing, tachypnea, and in acute exacerbations, open-mouth breathing with respiratory distress. Thoracic radiographs typically show a bronchial pattern; eosinophilia on bronchoalveolar lavage is confirmatory. Corticosteroids (systemic or inhaled) and bronchodilators (terbutaline, salbutamol) are the mainstays of management.

metadata.title: Feline bronchial disease: pathophysiology, diagnosis, and management
metadata.journal: Journal of Feline Medicine and Surgery

text: Inhaled corticosteroid therapy using a metered-dose inhaler with a feline spacer device (AeroKat) has become the preferred long-term management strategy for feline asthma, minimizing systemic side effects compared to oral prednisolone. Fluticasone propionate at 44–110 mcg per actuation given twice daily is a commonly reported protocol. A rescue bronchodilator (albuterol 90 mcg/puff) should be prescribed for acute episodes. Owners should be counseled on aerosol technique, spacer cleaning, and recognition of acute respiratory decompensation warranting emergency veterinary care. Environmental allergen reduction — including switching to low-dust litter and eliminating aerosol air fresheners — may reduce exacerbation frequency.

metadata.title: Feline bronchial disease: pathophysiology, diagnosis, and management
metadata.journal: Journal of Feline Medicine and Surgery

text: Brachycephalic obstructive airway syndrome (BOAS) is a multifactorial upper airway disease affecting dogs with congenitally shortened skulls, including Bulldogs, French Bulldogs, Pugs, Boston Terriers, and Shih Tzus. The syndrome comprises stenotic nares, elongated soft palate, hypoplastic trachea, and everted laryngeal saccules. Clinical signs range from stertor and snoring during rest to respiratory distress during exercise or in hot weather. Obesity significantly worsens signs. Diagnosis is based on clinical examination, awake assessment of nares, and laryngoscopy under sedation. Surgical correction of stenotic nares and shortened soft palate significantly improves clinical signs and quality of life, particularly when performed before the age of 2 years.

metadata.title: Brachycephalic obstructive airway syndrome: current concepts and surgical management
metadata.authors: Roedler FS, Pohl S, Oechtering GU

text: Pleural effusion in cats causes respiratory distress by compressing pulmonary parenchyma and reducing tidal volume. Common causes include chylothorax, pyothorax, cardiac disease (particularly hypertrophic cardiomyopathy), and neoplasia (especially lymphoma). Clinical signs include tachypnea, orthopnea (preference for sternal recumbency), open-mouth breathing, and cyanosis in severe cases. Thoracocentesis provides immediate therapeutic and diagnostic benefit. Fluid analysis (cytology, culture, triglyceride and cholesterol levels, and comparison with serum values) guides diagnosis. Chylothorax management includes dietary modification (low-fat diet), rutin supplementation, and surgical intervention (thoracic duct ligation with pericardectomy) for refractory cases.

metadata.title: Pleural effusion in cats: etiology, diagnostics, and management outcomes

text: Canine bacterial pneumonia most commonly results from aspiration of oropharyngeal contents or hematogenous spread. Predisposing factors include megaesophagus, laryngeal paralysis, vomiting disorders, anesthesia without airway protection, and immunosuppression. Gram-negative organisms (Escherichia coli, Klebsiella, Pasteurella) and anaerobes predominate in aspiration pneumonia. Clinical signs include productive cough, pyrexia, lethargy, nasal discharge, and tachypnea. Thoracic radiographs reveal an alveolar pattern, classically in the right middle and cranial lung lobes with aspiration. Diagnosis is confirmed by transtracheal wash or bronchoalveolar lavage with culture and sensitivity. Broad-spectrum antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate or fluoroquinolone with metronidazole) are administered pending culture results.

metadata.title: Aspiration pneumonia in dogs: risk factors, microbiology, and treatment outcomes
metadata.authors: Kogan DA, Johnson LR, Sturges BK
